steps.title: Find y-intercept and estimate slope from visual data
steps.content:
The data points range from $x \approx 1.0$ with prices around \$180–\$230 thousand, to $x \approx 3.0$ with prices around \$330–\$350 thousand. All data points suggest a positive y-intercept, eliminating choice D ($y = 100x$, which has y-intercept $0$). The remaining choices A, B, C have y-intercept $100$.
To estimate slope, identify the center of data at two regions:
  • At $x \approx 1.0$: center $\approx 210$
  • At $x \approx 2.5$: center $\approx 340$
Slope: $$\text{slope} \approx \frac{340 - 210}{2.5 - 1.0} = \frac{130}{1.5} \approx 87$$ This is closest to $100$, not $200$ or $50$.

steps.title: Verify slope of 100 matches the data
steps.content:
Backsolving — testing choice B ($y = 100x + 100$):
  • At $x = 1$: $y = 200$ (observed $\approx 210$) ✓
  • At $x = 2$: $y = 300$ (observed $\approx 300$) ✓
  • At $x = 2.5$: $y = 350$ (observed $\approx 340$) ✓
Compare to choice A (slope $200$): at $x = 2$, it predicts $y = 500$, far above the data. Compare to choice C (slope $50$): at $x = 2$, it predicts $y = 200$, below most data. The slope of $100$ provides the best fit. That matches choice B — B is correct.
